context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on this problem set, especially the graph coloring stuff. Could we start with Problem 1, but maybe very generally because I’m not even sure what kind of counterexample I’m supposed to be looking for?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's start there. Before thinking about a counterexample, let's make sure you understand what the claim is actually saying. In your own words, what does it mean for a graph to be '6-colorable'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feels lost. I should acknowledge their starting point and use a Socratic question to activate prior knowledge rather than explaining anything directly. I want to keep it bite-sized and direct them to think about what the claim actually says before worrying about finding a counterexample.

exchanges.student: I think it means I can assign one of 6 colors to each vertex so adjacent vertices get different colors, but I’m not fully sure what that rules out in practice. Also I’m kind of blanking on what “at most \(3n-6\) edges” should make me think of here.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's exactly right on 6-colorability. Now, the 3n-6 edge bound — does that formula ring any bells from your notes or textbook? Think about what class of graphs satisfies e ≤ 3n-6.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right definition of 6-colorability. I should affirm that briefly and then focus their attention on the 3n-6 edge bound, which is the key constraint. This should remind them of planar graphs, a concept they should know from the course. A single Socratic question pointing in that direction is enough.

exchanges.student: It vaguely reminds me of planar graphs, but I don’t remember if it’s “all planar graphs” or “only connected planar graphs” or if it’s just some random inequality. Honestly could you just give me the big-picture story here, because I feel like I’m missing the whole point before even doing the problem.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track — planar graphs are exactly what 3n-6 should make you think of. So here's a question: if every planar graph satisfies e ≤ 3n-6, what does that tell you about the relationship between the claim in Problem 1 and planar graphs?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the big picture, which would risk giving too much scaffolding. However, they are close to the right answer on planar graphs — I should affirm that and ask a targeted question to help them pin down the exact relationship, rather than launching into an explanation. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I guess the fake claim is trying to extend a true planar-graph statement to every graph that happens to satisfy the same edge bound, even if it isn’t planar. I still don’t see what kind of nonplanar example would force more than 6 colors though, and I’m tempted to just want a list of possible examples to choose from.
exchanges.tutor: Good insight — that's exactly the right framing. Now, to falsify the claim you need a graph that (1) has at most 3n-6 edges and (2) cannot be 6-colored. What's the minimum chromatic number a graph would need to have to violate the 6-coloring claim?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good conceptual leap — they understand the claim is about graphs satisfying the edge bound, not just planar graphs. I should affirm that. But they want a list of examples handed to them, which I should refuse. Instead, I should redirect them with a Socratic question: what is the minimum chromatic number that would falsify the claim, and are there any 'famous' graphs they know with high chromatic number?

exchanges.student: It would need chromatic number at least 7. I’m still not seeing how to search for one efficiently though, and I kind of want the broad method instead of just one tiny hint.
exchanges.tutor: Right, χ ≥ 7 is what you need. Rather than searching broadly, think about the simplest family of graphs with known chromatic number. What is χ(K_n), and does K_n satisfy the edge bound for any relevant value of n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identifies χ ≥ 7 as the requirement. They're asking for a broad method, which risks me doing too much of the thinking for them. I should redirect with a more targeted Socratic question: rather than searching for an exotic graph, can they think of a simple family of graphs with known chromatic number that they could check the edge count on? Complete graphs are the obvious candidate here.

exchanges.student: I know complete graphs have \(\chi(K_n)=n\), so I guess I should check \(K_7\). But I’m not confident doing the edge comparison, and I’m also worried there’s some trick and I’d rather just see the whole reasoning laid out cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: K_7 is a great candidate to check. You don't need a trick here — just compute: how many edges does K_7 have, and what does 3n-6 give you when n=7? Then compare the two numbers.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified K_7 as the candidate, which is exactly right. They're asking me to lay out the full reasoning, which I must refuse. I should push them to do the edge count themselves — it's a straightforward calculation. I'll ask them directly to compute the number of edges in K_7 and compare to 3n-6.

exchanges.student: For \(K_7\), the number of edges is \(\binom{7}{2}=21\), and \(3n-6=15\). So that one doesn’t fit. That didn’t work; I’m trying a different angle: is there some standard 7-chromatic graph with fewer edges, or am I supposed to somehow modify \(K_7\)?
exchanges.tutor: Good calculation. Here's something worth thinking about: if you remove an edge from K_7, does the chromatic number necessarily drop below 7? What would have to be true about the two endpoints of that edge for the chromatic number to stay at 7?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed that K_7 has too many edges. They're now thinking in the right direction — either find a sparser 7-chromatic graph or modify K_7. Rather than pointing them to a specific graph, I should ask them to think about whether they need exactly χ=7, or if they can think bigger. Actually, the key insight is that K_8 has even more edges, so going up won't help. The real question is: do they need χ≥7, or could they think about this differently? Wait — actually the problem just asks for a graph that cannot be 6-colored, so χ≥7 suffices. But perhaps more importantly, I should ask them to reconsider: is K_7 the only way to get χ=7? Could they think about what modifications preserve high chromatic number while reducing edges? But I don't want to over-scaffold. Let me ask them to think about what happens if they remove edges from K_7 — does chromatic number necessarily drop?

exchanges.student: I’m not sure, and this is where I start getting tangled. Could you just explain the candidate I should use and why it works, because I feel like I’m circling without getting anywhere.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give it away, but let me redirect you since you're circling. You correctly showed K_7 has too many edges for n=7. But n doesn't have to stay at 7 — what happens to the bound 3n-6 if you increase n by adding vertices to K_7 without adding any edges between them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and asking for the answer directly, which I must refuse. However, they may genuinely be stuck. Rather than continuing down the edge-removal path which is getting complicated, I should redirect them. Actually, the simplest counterexample is just K_7 itself — wait, that doesn't satisfy the edge bound. Let me reconsider the problem. The claim says 'at most 3n-6 edges implies 6-colorable.' A counterexample needs a graph with ≤3n-6 edges and χ>6. K_7 has 21 edges and 3(7)-6=15, so it doesn't work. What about larger complete graphs? K_n has n(n-1)/2 edges, and 3n-6 grows linearly while edges grow quadratically, so for large n complete graphs always exceed the bound. The student needs a different approach. Actually, thinking more carefully: one standard counterexample is to take a graph like K_7 and note it doesn't satisfy the bound, but what about a graph that is NOT complete but still has χ=7? This is non-trivial. Actually, re-reading the problem: it says the FALSE claim is that 'every graph with n nodes and at most 3n-6 edges can be 6-colored.' The student just needs ONE graph violating this. Since the student is going in circles, I should redirect them to think about it from a different angle: instead of starting from a high-chromatic graph and reducing edges, start from the edge bound and ask what kinds of graphs satisfy it. Planar graphs always satisfy it, and the 4-color theorem says planar graphs are 4-colorable, so planar graphs won't give χ>6. But non-planar graphs can also satisfy 3n-6 sometimes. Actually, I realize the simplest approach might be: take a complete graph K_7, it doesn't satisfy the bound. But what if n is large? For K_n, edges = n(n-1)/2, and 3n-6 grows slower, so complete graphs never work for large n either. Hmm. Let me think of another approach. What about graphs that are constructed to have high chromatic number with few edges? Actually, the Mycielski construction gives triangle-free graphs with high chromatic number. The Mycielskian of K_2 gives a sequence: K_2 (χ=2), C_5 (χ=3), Grötzsch graph (χ=4, 11 vertices, 20 edges... 3(11)-6=27, so 20≤27 ✓), then the next Mycielskian has χ=5... This could work but is complex. Actually wait — let me reconsider. Maybe the simplest counterexample uses a known result. What about the complete bipartite graph? No, that's 2-colorable. What about odd cycles? Those are 3-colorable. Hmm. What about the Petersen graph? χ=3. Let me think differently. The problem says 'find some integer n' — so n can be anything. What if we use a disjoint union of complete graphs? Like, many copies of K_7? Each K_7 has 7 nodes and 21 edges, but 3(7)-6=15 < 21, so each component violates the bound. What about taking n copies of K_7? That gives 7n nodes and 21n edges, while 3(7n)-6 = 21n-6 < 21n. Still doesn't work. What about K_8? 28 edges, 3(8)-6=18. Nope. Hmm. Actually, I wonder if the problem is pointing to a specific construction from the recitation. The recitation problem 3 proved that planar graphs are 6-colorable using the fact that planar graphs have a vertex of degree ≤5, which follows from e≤3n-6. The false claim extends this to ALL graphs with e≤3n-6. So the question is: is there a non-planar graph with e≤3n-6 that has χ>6? Actually, I think the key insight the problem is going for might be simpler. Let me reconsider. Maybe they want the student to realize that for DISCONNECTED graphs, 3n-6 can be larger relative to edges. For example, a graph consisting of many disjoint K_7's: if we have k copies of K_7, n=7k nodes, edges=21k, 3n-6=21k-6. So edges=21k > 21k-6=3n-6. Still doesn't satisfy the bound by 6. What if we add isolated vertices? Take K_7 plus (k) isolated vertices: n=7+k, edges=21, 3n-6=3(7+k)-6=15+3k. For k≥2, 15+3k≥21, so the bound is satisfied! And χ=7 since K_7 is a subgraph. So: take K_7 plus 2 isolated vertices (n=9, edges=21, 3n-6=21). That works! Or even K_7 plus 1 isolated vertex: n=8, edges=21, 3(8)-6=18 < 21. Doesn't work. K_7 plus 2 isolated vertices: n=9, edges=21, 3(9)-6=21. 21≤21 ✓. And χ=7>6. This is the counterexample! So the approach is: take K_7 and pad with isolated vertices to make the edge bound satisfied. I should guide the student toward this without giving it away. The student is frustrated and circling. I should give a more direct hint without giving the answer. I can ask: what if instead of modifying K_7 by removing edges, they think about adding vertices?

exchanges.student: Oh, so I could take \(K_7\) plus some isolated vertices. Then the graph still needs 7 colors because of the \(K_7\) part, but \(n\) gets bigger while the number of edges stays 21. I think that means I just need \(21 \le 3n-6\), but I’d like help picking the smallest \(n\) and writing that up without making it clumsy.
exchanges.tutor: Great insight! Now just solve 21 ≤ 3n-6 for the smallest integer n — that's all the algebra you need. The writeup is entirely yours to do; I can't help polish or structure it. What do you get for the minimum n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has had the key insight on their own — excellent. They now just need to solve 21 ≤ 3n-6 for the smallest n, which is straightforward algebra. I should not do this for them or help them write it up (per the rules: I must not write or suggest language). I'll just point them to the algebra and remind them the writeup is entirely their responsibility.

exchanges.student: I get \(27 \le 3n\), so \(n \ge 9\), and the smallest is \(n=9\). I’m also fuzzy on part (b), though, and honestly I’d rather get a full intuitive explanation of why the induction breaks instead of another tiny question.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — n=9 works. Now for part (b): induction proofs on graphs typically remove a vertex, apply the hypothesis to the smaller graph, then reinsert the vertex. What condition on the smaller graph needs to hold for the inductive hypothesis to apply, and can you guarantee it still holds after removing a vertex?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly solved for n=9, which is right. I should affirm this briefly. On part (b), they're asking for a full intuitive explanation, which I must refuse. Instead, I should ask a targeted Socratic question to get them thinking about where induction proofs typically break down — specifically, the inductive step usually requires removing a vertex and applying the inductive hypothesis, which requires the remaining graph to still satisfy the hypothesis's conditions.
